summary: Cantrill reflects on how boom/bust cycles are endemic across industries, from his grandfather's petroleum engineering career derailed by the oil bust to his own experience in software during the Dot-Com crash. He initially thought software would be 'bust-proof' since it wasn't tied to physical resources, but learned that tech cycles are if anything more extreme. Despite this, he still prefers software's volatility to other industries since at least there aren't literal ghost towns yet.
key_points: Boom/bust cycles are endemic across industries, from oil to mining to software
The author naively believed software would be 'bust-proof' since it wasn't tied to physical commodities
The Dot-Com bust proved tech cycles can be even more extreme than resource-based industries
Software companies can rise and fall faster than those in traditional industries
money_quotes: So when, in the early 1990s, I realized that my life's calling was in software, I felt a sense of relief: here was an industry that was at last not shackled to the fickle Earth – an industry that was surely 'bust-proof' at some level. Ha!
As I learned (painfully, like most everyone else) in the Dot-Com boom and subsequent bust, booms and busts are – if anything – even more endemic in our industry.
Companies grow from nothing to spectacular heights in virtually no time at all – and can crash back into nothingness just as quickly.
I'll still take our boom and bust cycles over those in other industries: there are, after all, still not quite yet software 'ghost towns'
key_insight: Software's intangibility doesn't insulate it from boom/bust cycles – if anything, the lack of physical constraints makes tech companies rise and fall even faster than traditional industries.
